taxonomy: Calendula officinalis, commonly known as Pot Marigold, is a member of the Asteraceae family, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants. This classification is supported by a confidence score of 10, indicating a high level of certainty in its familial assignment. As a member of the Asteraceae family, Calendula officinalis is closely related to other plants such as daisies and sunflowers.
identification: Calendula officinalis, also known as pot marigold, is a flowering plant that can be identified by its bright yellow and orange petals with a distinctive shape and arrangement. It typically grows to be around 1-3 feet in height, with a branching stem and coarse, green leaves that are lance-shaped and pointed at the tip. The plant's flowers often bloom in a spiral pattern, and are usually 2-3 inches in diameter, adding to its easily recognizable appearance.
uses: Calendula officinalis is used to treat a variety of ailments, including skin issues such as wounds, burns, and eczema, due to its anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties. It is also effective in soothing digestive issues, including constipation, by promoting regular bowel movements and reducing inflammation in the gut. Additionally, calendula officinalis has been used to reduce inflammation and promote healing in cases of hemorrhoids. Furthermore, it is known to alleviate symptoms of irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) by regulating bowel movements and reducing abdominal pain. Calendula officinalis has also been used to treat minor injuries such as scrapes and cuts, due to its ability to promote wound healing and tissue repair.
benefits: Calendula officinalis is used to treat a variety of ailments, including skin irritations, wounds, and minor burns, thanks to its soothing and anti-inflammatory properties. This plant is beneficial for promoting wound healing, reducing inflammation and pain, and even helping to prevent scarring. Its health benefits also extend to improving digestive issues, such as irritable bowel syndrome and diarrhea, as well as reducing symptoms of menopause and menstrual cramps in women. Additionally, calendula officinalis has been shown to have antimicrobial properties, making it useful for preventing infections and promoting healthy skin, hair, and mucous membranes.
properties: Calendula officinalis has many therapeutic properties, such as its antiseptic properties, which help to prevent infection and promote wound healing by inhibiting the growth of bacteria and other microorganisms. Additionally, the anti-inflammatory properties of calendula officinalis make it an effective remedy for reducing swelling and pain associated with various skin conditions. The antioxidant properties of calendula officinalis also help to protect cells from damage caused by free radicals, thereby promoting overall skin health and preventing premature aging. Furthermore, the analgesic properties of calendula officinalis make it a popular natural remedy for alleviating pain and discomfort associated with burns, cuts, and other skin irritations. The antimicrobial properties of calendula officinalis also help to eliminate fungal and bacterial infections, making it a valuable treatment for conditions such as acne and eczema.
constituents: Calendula officinalis has been found to contain a diverse array of bioactive constituents, including triterpenoid saponins, which exhibit anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, while also possessing antioxidant and immunomodulatory effects. The flavonoids present in this plant, such as quercetin and kaempferol, have been shown to possess anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and anti-cancer properties. Additionally, calendula officinalis contains phenolic acids like caffeic acid and ferulic acid, which have been demonstrated to possess antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory activities. Furthermore, the plant's essential oil contains sesquiterpenes like caryophyllene and borneol, which have been found to exhibit antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, as well as to act as a local anesthetic.
parts: The most commonly used parts of Calendula officinalis are its leaves, flowers, and roots. The leaves are used to treat skin conditions such as eczema, acne, and dermatitis due to their antimicrobial properties, and are often made into teas or infused in oils for topical application. The flowers are a key component in herbal remedies, used to soothe and heal wounds, reduce inflammation, and promote tissue repair, and are commonly used in creams, ointments, and salves for skin issues like sunburns and minor cuts. The roots, although less frequently used, are also employed in traditional medicine to treat conditions such as fever, digestive issues, and respiratory problems, often in the form of tinctures or decoctions.
preparations: Calendula officinalis most common herbal preparations are infusions, tinctures, salves, and creams. These preparations are used for various health purposes, including wound care and skin issues, where the infusions and tinctures are often used to treat conditions such as eczema, acne, and minor burns, while the salves and creams are applied topically to soothe and calm irritated skin. Additionally, calendula preparations are also used in the management of menstrual cramps, sore throats, and digestive issues, where the infusions are often consumed as a tea to promote relaxation and reduce inflammation. Furthermore, calendula creams and salves are also applied to reduce the appearance of scars, stretch marks, and to promote wound healing, making it a popular choice for natural skincare and beauty routines.
side_effects: Calendula officinalis side effects are primarily experienced by individuals with sensitive skin or allergies, who may react to its potent properties. These side effects can include skin irritation, redness, itching, burning sensations, stinging, and rashes, as well as digestive issues such as nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and stomach cramps. In rare cases, people may experience more severe reactions, including anaphylaxis, hives, and respiratory problems, especially those with pre-existing conditions such as asthma or eczema. To minimize the risk of side effects, it is essential to use calendula products in moderation, avoid applying them to broken skin, and perform a patch test before using them extensively.
title: calendula officinalis
intro: Calendula officinalis, also known as pot marigold, is a flowering plant that belongs to the daisy family and is native to the Mediterranean region. The plant's flowers, leaves, and stalks contain anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, making it a popular herbal remedy for various health issues. Calendula officinalis has been traditionally used to treat wounds, skin conditions, such as eczema and acne, and to soothe digestive issues due to its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. The plant can be prepared into herbal teas, salves, creams, and ointments, and its medicinal properties can also be extracted into oils and tinctures for topical or internal use.
actions: Calendula officinalis has anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antiseptic properties that primarily affect the integumentary system, wound healing, and skin conditions. Its vulnerary, cicatrizant, and protective actions also impact the skin's ability to repair and regenerate itself. Additionally, calendula's antifungal, antiviral, and antioxidant properties influence the immune system's response to infection and damage. Furthermore, its soothing, demulcent, and anti-edematous effects contribute to the plant's overall impact on the integumentary system, reducing inflammation and promoting tissue repair.
intro_study: According to Farmakologiia i toksikologiia, Calendula officinalis has medicinal purposes in treating ulcers, exhibiting an antiulcerous action in experimental models.
benefits_desc: Calendula officinalis is used to promote overall health and well-being, with numerous benefits ranging from soothing skin irritations and wound healing to reducing inflammation and relieving pain. This versatile plant has been shown to be effective in treating various health issues, including eczema, acne, and rosacea, while also supporting immune function and fighting off infections. The health benefits of calendula officinalis also extend to reducing symptoms of menopause, such as hot flashes and night sweats, as well as providing relief from menstrual cramps and other symptoms associated with PMS. Additionally, calendula officinalis has been used to support eye health, reduce the appearance of fine lines and wrinkles, and even help to prevent the spread of cancer cells.

Its antispasmodic and vulnerary actions also affect the integumentary system, promoting tissue repair and reducing inflammation. Additionally, calendula officinalis exhibits anti-arthritic and anti-rheumatic properties, affecting the musculoskeletal system and providing relief from joint pain and inflammation. Furthermore, its antifungal and antiviral properties also demonstrate its therapeutic actions on the integumentary and lymphatic systems, offering protection against infections and promoting immune system balance.

constituents_desc: Calendula officinalis has been found to contain a diverse array of bioactive constituents, including flavonoids, such as quercetin and kaempferol, which possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. Tannins, like gallic acid and ellagic acid, present in the plant, exhibit antimicrobial and astringent activities, while triterpenoids, including ursolic acid and oleanolic acid, demonstrate anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory effects. The plant also contains sesquiterpenes, like calendulol and isocalendulol, which display antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, as well as phenolic acids, including ferulic acid and sinapic acid, which exhibit antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities. Additionally, the plant's bioactive constituents include phytosterols, such as beta-sitosterol and stigmasterol, which may possess anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory effects.
parts_desc: The most commonly used parts of Calendula officinalis are its flowers, leaves, and roots. The flowers, rich in carotenoids and flavonoids, are typically used to create topical creams, ointments, and salves for wound healing, skin irritations, and inflammatory conditions such as eczema and acne. The leaves and stems, which contain a higher concentration of saponins, are often used to make teas and infusions for their antimicrobial and antifungal properties, providing relief from digestive issues and respiratory problems like bronchitis and coughs. The roots, rich in sesquiterpenes, are less commonly used but still utilized to create remedies for their antibacterial and anti-inflammatory effects, often applied externally to treat skin conditions and wounds.
preparations_desc: Calendula officinalis most common herbal preparations are infusions, tinctures, salves, and creams. Infusions, also known as teas, are often used to treat digestive issues such as irritable bowel syndrome, as well as to reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms of colds and flu. Calendula tinctures are commonly used topically to accelerate wound healing, reduce inflammation, and soothe skin irritations like acne, eczema, and sunburn, while also being used internally to aid in digestion and relieve menstrual cramps. Calendula salves and creams are typically used to treat minor burns, cuts, and scrapes, as well as to soothe and moisturize dry skin, chapped lips, and irritated eyes.

side_effects_desc: Calendula officinalis side effects are typically mild and rare, but may include skin irritation, allergic reactions, and contact dermatitis in some individuals, especially those with sensitive skin. People with allergies, eczema, or other skin conditions are more likely to experience these side effects, such as redness, itching, swelling, and blistering. Pregnant or breastfeeding women may also be at a higher risk of experiencing side effects like nausea, headaches, and dizziness due to the potential estrogenic activity of calendula. Additionally, individuals taking medications such as blood thinners, diabetes medications, or blood pressure medications should exercise caution when using calendula products, as they may interact with these medications and cause side effects like bruising, bleeding, or changes in blood sugar levels.

side_effects_list.name: skin irritation
side_effects_list.desc: calendula officinalis can cause skin irritation in some individuals, especially if used in high concentrations or without proper dilution.

side_effects_list.desc: some people may be allergic to calendula, which can cause an allergic reaction, including symptoms such as hives, itching, and swelling.

side_effects_list.desc: ingesting calendula can cause digestive issues, such as nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea, in some individuals.

side_effects_list.name: interactions with medications
side_effects_list.desc: calendula may interact with certain medications, such as blood thinners, diabetes medications, and blood pressure medications, which can lead to adverse effects.

side_effects_list.name: eye irritation
side_effects_list.desc: calendula extracts can cause eye irritation, including redness, itching, and tearing, if not handled properly.

side_effects_list.name: photosensitivity
side_effects_list.desc: calendula may cause photosensitivity in some individuals, leading to skin reactions when exposed to sunlight.

side_effects_list.name: pregnancy and breastfeeding concerns
side_effects_list.desc: there is limited research on the safety of calendula during pregnancy and breastfeeding, so it's recommended to consult with a healthcare professional before using it.
